fields.narrative_intro: *A hyperventilating person and a nerdy person discuss Claude Opus 4.8 in a metaverse that has no users.*
fields.panel1:
[setup]
NERDY PERSON: "Opus 4.8 is 61% cheaper than 4.7 per Databricks."
HYPERVENTILATING PERSON: "So frontier AI doesn't have to cost a fortune?"
fields.panel2:
[build]
NERDY PERSON: "84% on Mind2Web. Beats GPT-5.5. 2.5× faster in Fast mode."
HYPERVENTILATING PERSON: "What's the catch?"
fields.panel3:
[pivot]
NERDY PERSON: "It's also 4× more likely to call out your bad code."
HYPERVENTILATING PERSON: "...my code?"
fields.panel4:
[silent reaction]
[Visual: The hyperventilating person slowly rotates their laptop screen away from the nerdy person. Behind them, a vast empty metaverse plaza stretches to the horizon — no users, just tumbleweeds made of pixel art.]
fields.panel5:
[punch]
HYPERVENTILATING PERSON: "What if I just... use the expensive slow one."
NERDY PERSON: "That's not how cheaper works."
fields.panel6:
[button]
[Visual: The laptop screen is now fully facing a wall. On it, Claude's review reads: '47 critical issues found.' A single Harvey legal-benchmark trophy sits in the corner, glowing smugly at 10%.]

narrative_summary: A nerdy person rattles off Opus 4.8's impressive cost and benchmark stats until the hyperventilating person realizes the model's new code-flaw-catching ability threatens their own terrible code, and tries to opt back into expensive ignorance.
rationale: Candidate 5 earns its punchline by pivoting from the story's headline stats to a genuinely fresh angle — the user's own bad code as the real casualty of improvement — and the laptop-facing-the-wall visual gag pays off the setup rather than restating it. The '47 critical issues found' button is specific, earned, and funny without being obvious.
place: in a metaverse that has no users
hat_pair: crash helmet with a tiny windshield wiper on the visor
full-face motorcycle helmet with googly eyes stuck on
